I don’t have direct access to exp8_csv_small/csv/smart_agriculture_drone_imagery_1_1.csv, but I can explain how the listed hyper-parameters typically shape per-row NDVI values and timestamps, and why a different hyper_param_index would yield a different CSV.

How the hyper-parameters influence each row
- seasonality_amplitude
  - Role: Controls how strongly NDVI varies with the seasonal cycle (e.g., higher NDVI in growing seasons, lower in off-season).
  - Effect on per-row NDVI: NDVI values will follow a seasonal pattern (often a sine-like wave) with a peak-to-trough range set by the amplitude. Higher amplitude means more pronounced highs and lows at different times of year.
  - Effect on timestamps: Generally indirect; the seasonal timing is tied to the timestamp, so changing amplitude changes NDVI shapes but not the actual dates.

- anomaly_rate
  - Role: Sets how often anomalies occur.
  - Effect on per-row NDVI: A higher rate yields more occasional outliers (sudden spikes or drops) superimposed on the baseline/seasonal NDVI.
  - Effect on timestamps: Typically none; anomalies affect the value at a given timestamp, not the time itself.

- anomaly_magnitude
  - Role: Determines how large each anomaly is.
  - Effect on per-row NDVI: Greater magnitude produces more extreme deviations from the baseline/seasonal pattern when an anomaly occurs.
  - Effect on timestamps: None.

- uplift_on_ndvi
  - Role: Provides a global offset to NDVI values.
  - Effect on per-row NDVI: Shifts the entire NDVI series upward (or downward) by a fixed amount, raising or lowering the baseline mean.
  - Effect on timestamps: None.

- cloud_cover_mean
  - Role: Simulates the influence of cloud cover on imagery, often affecting observed NDVI.
  - Effect on per-row NDVI: Higher cloud cover reduces the observed NDVI (either via a multiplicative dampening or an additional noise/attenuation term), and may introduce occasional masked or dampened values.
  - Effect on timestamps: Typically none; cloud cover affects the measured value rather than the time points themselves.

Why running the generator with a different hyper_param_index would yield a different CSV
- The generator uses hyper_param_index (out of total_hyper_params) to select a specific set of parameter values (seasonality_amplitude, anomaly_rate, anomaly_magnitude, uplift_on_ndvi, cloud_cover_mean, etc.).
- With total_hyper_params > 1, each index would correspond to a different combination of these parameters, producing a distinct NDVI trajectory and possibly different masking/dampening by cloud cover, resulting in a different CSV.
- Even if timestamps are generated with a fixed cadence, a different parameter set can shift the NDVI shape relative to the same timestamps. If the generator also allows starting date or cadence to be affected by the hyper-parameter set, timestamps could differ as well.

What you can check or provide to get a precise mapping
- If you can share a small sample of the CSV (a few rows) or the header columns, I can map each column to the above factors and describe exactly how the given values reflect seasonality, anomalies, uplift, and cloud effects.
- Check for metadata or a header/footer in the CSV or a separate config file that lists the hyperparameter values for hyper_param_index=1. If you share those values, I can illustrate the exact expected NDVI and timestamp behavior for this specific run.
- To compare runs, generate another CSV with a different hyper_param_index (e.g., index 0 or 2) and compare:
  - NDVI value patterns (seasonal shape, anomaly occurrences, overall uplift)
  - The distribution of NDVI values (mean, min/max)
  - Any changes in timestamps (start date, cadence)

If you’d like, paste a few rows (including the timestamp and NDVI column names) and I’ll tailor the explanation to your exact data.
